title: My Best Dividend Aristocrats For March 2023
text: NOBL started the year off with a gain of 3.23% in January; it is thus far not faring as well in February, but the ETF is still positive year-to-date. I present 3 strategies that can theoretically beat the dividend aristocrat index in the long term.

title: AMG vs. TROW: Which Stock Is the Better Value Option?
text: AMG vs. TROW: Which Stock Is the Better Value Option?

title: Dividend Increases Are Flying In This Month!
text: The dividend increases continue to roll in during February. A ton of companies have announced dividend increases. Dividend increases are a critical part of our dividend growth investing strategy. We focus on buying dividend stocks to build a growing passive income stream.

title: 5 Stocks In The Dividend Growth Sweet Spot
text: Finding companies with a high initial yield and rapid dividend growth is difficult. I present my criteria for finding such companies. At present, there are 16 companies meeting my initial screening requirements. Using additional metrics, these are reduced to five.

title: 7 Stocks That Wall Street Hates — and YOU Should Buy!
text: While it's usually not the wisest move to trade against Wall Street experts, sometimes, they overlook certain ideas, presenting contrarian opportunities among stocks to buy. To be sure, the politics of the investment analysis world dictates that experts lean more bullishly; otherwise, it's difficult to establish relationships with corporate movers and shakers.

title: Is This High-Yielding Stock Still a Buy?
text: This asset manager boasts decades of consecutive dividend growth.

title: 4 Dividend Aristocrat Buys For February
text: "Dividend Aristocrats in the S&P 500 Index have raised their annual payouts every year for at least 25 consecutive years." - Kiplinger.com/Investing. By yield, VFC topped all Aristocrats. Top-ten yields from TROW, ESS, FRT, AMCR, ABBV, O, IBM, MMM, WBA, & VFC, averaged 4.38%.

title: T. Rowe Price: A 16% Total Return Layup
text: T. Rowe Price has been increasing dividends for 36 consecutive years. Shares are historically priced at the 3% yield mark. Today, it is available at above 4% yield. The shares are depressed because revenues are calculated as a percentage of financial assets, which are correlated to the market. Once the market recovers, so will revenues.
